content.full_text:
August 30, 1990

Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, 8th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Jonathan:

This letter will confirm our telephone conversation on August 27, 1990. As we discussed, this firm serves as legal counsel to Californians Against 130, a committee primarily formed to oppose Proposition 130 on the November ballot. We discussed two issues relative to Proposition 105 application.

First, is the descriptive phrase "Forest Products Industry" sufficient to describe the committee's major funding sources which currently come primarily from four companies, namely Sierra Pacific, Georgia Pacific, Louisiana Pacific and Pacific Lumber Company. We discussed the fact that these companies own timber tracts, harvest timber, mill lumber and make paper and other forestry products. We also reviewed the proposed regulation regarding industry and the fact that these companies could be designated under several different major categories (Forestry, Lumber & Wood Products, Paper & Allied Products) and sub-categories. You informed me that the Commission would review requests like this one and determine whether an industry description is "reasonable" or "unreasonable" based on the facts provided. You advised me that "Forest Products Industry" was a reasonable description for these companies. Note that "Forest Products" is both a three-digit sub-category and a four-digit sub-category.

Second, the committee has no report due until October 5, 1990, and has not filed any report because it was formed after the last filing period; nonetheless, the committee would like to file a voluntary campaign report upon which it will base its Proposition 105 disclosure. You agreed that we could file a campaign report at any time pursuant to Government Code section 84511.

Thank you for your consideration on these issues.

Sincerely,

[Signature]
THOMAS W. HILTACHK
